message.content:
### 1) Working/Short/Long Memory Layering
Layer memory hierarchically: **short-term (working) memory** holds immediate task context (e.g., current conversation turns) via summarization and compaction to avoid overflow; **long-term memory** stores stable facts, user state, and milestones in structured formats like summaries or notes for retrieval.[1][2][3] Use a central **context manager** to condense transcripts into running state descriptions (e.g., decisions, goals) and feed role-specific views to agents—reasoning agents get structured data, response agents get summaries.[1][2] For long-horizon tasks, combine compaction for flow, note-taking for iterations, and multi-agent handoffs for persistence.[2]

### 2) Graph-First Retrieval with Fallbacks
Prioritize **graph-based retrieval** (e.g., knowledge graphs for entity relationships) as primary for precise, structured recall, falling back to semantic search or RAG for broader queries.[1][3] Design retrieval around observed context patterns: discover from behavior, build graph stores for user state/actions/threads, and use agent-specific formatting.[1] Fallbacks include runtime tool use (e.g., search APIs) with heuristics to avoid dead-ends; pre-compute where possible for speed over exploration.[2][3]

### 3) Token and Latency Control
Keep context **tight and informative**: summarize aggressively, avoid appending full histories, and measure token usage per step.[1][2][3] Optimize via **compaction** (condense old details), structured storage (edit/audit facts), and relevance filtering—target <80% window fill for pollution resistance.[2][3] Reduce latency with single-agent for linear tasks, hierarchical multi-agent for parallel work, and pre-retrieval over runtime exploration.[2][3]

### 4) Cross-Terminal/Session Continuity
Maintain continuity via **persistent context managers** tracking user state, threads, and actions across sessions/terminals.[1][3] Store in auditable long-term memory (avoid sensitive data without consent); retrieve via graphs or summaries on resume.[1][3] Use orchestration layers for handoffs, human gates, and unified visibility to prevent sprawl.[1][4]

### 5) Common Failure Modes Observed by Teams
- **Context pollution/flakiness**: Ad-hoc prompting or unfiltered appending leads to irrelevant info overwhelming models—fix with standardization and compaction.[1][2]
- **Token overflow in long tasks**: Exceeding windows without layering causes incoherence—mitigate via summaries and multi-agent delegation.[2][3]
- **Retrieval misuse**: Agents chase dead-ends or ignore keys without heuristics/tools—add guidance and fallbacks.[2]
- **Agent sprawl**: Disconnected agents lack governance, causing unintended actions—use orchestration, audits, sandboxes.[3][4]
- **State loss**: Poor persistence breaks continuity—centralize managers and structured memory.[1][3]

Sources: [Sombra Guide](https://sombrainc.com/blog/ai-context-engineering-guide)[1], [Anthropic Engineering](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents)[2], [Stack AI 2026 Guide](https://www.stack-ai.com/blog/the-2026-guide-to-agentic-workflow-architectures)[3], [UiPath Adoption](https://www.uipath.com/blog/ai/adopting-agentic-ai-2026-things-you-can-do-right-now)[4].
